title: Databases — Where Data Lives
subtitle: SQL, tables, joins, transactions, and when to use NoSQL

sections.title: Tables, Rows & Relationships
sections.text:
Databases store data in tables — like spreadsheets with rows and columns. A users table has columns for id, name, email. Each row is one user.

Tables connect through foreign keys. An orders table points to user_id — one user has many orders. You can ask: "Give me all orders for this user" and the database figures it out instantly.

Indexes make lookups fast. Without one, finding "alice@example.com" scans every single row. With an index on email, the database jumps straight to the right row — O(log n) instead of O(n).

config.caption: users and orders tables connected by a foreign key — one user has many orders

sections.caption: Tables connected by foreign keys form the backbone of relational databases

sections.title: Joins & Transactions
sections.text:
Joins let you ask questions across multiple tables in one query. "Show me all orders with user names and email." The database matches rows between tables using foreign keys.

Transactions ensure that a group of operations all succeed or all fail together. Think of transferring money: subtract from account A AND add to account B — you can't do just one. If anything goes wrong, everything rolls back.

ACID is the guarantee: Atomic (all or nothing), Consistent (data follows rules), Isolated (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durable (written to disk safely).

items.heading: INNER JOIN
items.body: Rows that match in both tables. Most common join type.

items.heading: LEFT JOIN
items.body: All rows from left table + matching right rows. Nulls for no match.

items.heading: ACID Transactions
items.body: Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable — the four guarantees.

items.heading: N+1 Problem
items.body: Querying N rows + 1 query per row = N+1 queries. Use eager loading instead.

sections.caption: Key SQL concepts: joins combine tables, transactions ensure data integrity
sections.callout: Always use transactions for operations that must succeed or fail together. Money transfers, order creation, and account registration all need atomicity.

sections.title: SQL vs NoSQL
sections.text:
SQL databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL) are structured and strict. You define your schema upfront, and the database enforces it. Great for finance, e-commerce — anything where data integrity matters.

NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Redis) are flexible. Store whatever you want, add fields anytime. Great for prototyping, real-time features, and hierarchical data like comments or pages.

Which one? Start with PostgreSQL. It handles 95% of use cases. Add Redis for caching. Only reach for MongoDB if your data truly doesn't fit into tables.

items: Best for: real-time, IoT, content management

sections.caption: SQL for consistency and relationships, NoSQL for flexibility and scale
sections.detail: You don't have to choose one. Many production systems use both — PostgreSQL for transactional data and Redis for caching, or MongoDB for content and Kafka for event streams. This is called polyglot persistence.
